title: Build or Buy? Human Capital and Corporate Diversification
abstract: Firms enter new sectors by either building on their resources or buying existing companies. Using French administrative data, we propose a measure of human capital distance between a firm and a sector of entry. Using a shift-share instrument, we show that firms build in close sectors and buy in distant sectors in terms of human capital distance. Firms build by hiring new workers, which becomes increasingly costly in distant sectors as it requires not only hiring more workers but also having more organizational capital to integrate these workers. Hence, firms buy in distant sectors to acquire already operational human capital.

What are the barriers to corporate diversification? Theories of the firm, dating back toPenrose (1955), propose that firms diversify to leverage their existing resources across sectors. Consistent with this view, the literature has shown that firms typically enter sectors that require similar resources to those they already possess (Bernard, Redding, and Schott 2010;Hoberg and Phillips 2018;Boehm, Dhingra, and Morrow 2022). However, how do firms acquire the resources they lack to operate in a new sector? What are the costs of acquiring these resources? Answering these questions is key to understanding the determinants of corporate diversification, and by extension, firm growth.

This paper studies why some firms choose to develop their resources organically (“build”), while others prefer to acquire them by buying an incumbent company (“buy”). We focus on human capital (HC) as a crucial resource for firms seeking to diversify (e.g.,Teece 1982). We examine how firms develop the human capital to enter a new sector to shed light on the frictions faced by firms when expanding their scope.

Firms’ existing human capital influences which sectors they enter, as firms select sectors requiring similar human capital to minimize adjustment costs or maximize synergies (Neffke and Henning 2013;Tate and Yang 2024). However, whether human capital affects how firms enter a new sector depends on what developing the necessary human capital entails. If developing human capital only involves hiring new workers (i.e., identifying and attracting workers with the appropriate set of skills), firms could always build by poaching workers from incumbents to replicate their human capital, such that firms’ existing human capital would be irrelevant in the decision to build or buy. In contrast, if firms also need to invest to integrate new hires into their workforce (e.g., train, manage, and assign them to tasks; seePrescott and Visscher 1980), firms will opt to buy when their existing human capital does not align with the new sector. Unlike building, buying allows firms to acquire operational human capital and save on this aspect of integration costs, which is particularly valuable for firms with limited organizational skills.

Our main result is that firms are more likely to buy when they lack the human capital to operate in the new sector. This finding indicates that organically building human capital is costly and requires organizational skills, whereas buying allows firms with limited organizational skills to acquire already operational human capital and expand their scope faster.

We construct a novel data set of build and buy entries in France from 2003 to 2014. We rely on administrative data that contain the detailed breakdown of firm sales by sector to identify the universe of entries into new sectors. We use merger and acquisition (M&A) deals from SDC Platinum and Bureau van Dijk’s Zephyr to determine whether a firm enters by acquiring an existing company. The resulting data set consists of 61,000 build or buy entries, of which 98% are made by developing existing resources organically (85% when weighting by entry sales). The large fraction of build entries suggests that firms buy only when they cannot build in the new sector.

We develop a new measure of human capital distance between a firm and a potential sector of entry. Specifically, we use the French employer-employee data set, which includes a detailed occupation code at the individual employee level and the exact employment composition of all firms in France. FollowingLazear (2009), we define a firm’s human capital as a combination of occupation-specific skills. This definition has the advantage of encompassing several components of the human capital of the entire workforce, including education, experience, and sector-specific skills. Our measure of HC distance corresponds to the occupation overlap between the diversifying firm and incumbent firms in the sector of entry.1HC distance captures the importance of human capital investment that a firm needs to undertake to enter a new sector.

Our main finding is that firms build in close sectors and buy in more distant sectors in terms of HC distance. A one-standard-deviation increase in HC distance is associated with a 20% increase in the unconditional probability of firms buying. Our baseline specification compares firms with different HC levels that operate in the same sector of origin, but choose to enter that sector in different ways. Our specification controls for unobservable, time-varying factors that affect firms’ choice to build or buy within sector pairs, as well as other possible firm-level determinants of the decision to build or buy, such as firm size, profitability, capital intensity, and access to liquidity (Matvos, Seru, and Silva 2018;Berg, Streitz, and Wedow 2024).2

Although our data allow us to include detailed firm-level control variables and granular fixed effects, it is still possible that other time-varying unobservable factors impact both HC distance and the decision of firms to build or buy. We adopt a shift-share design (Bartik 1991;Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin, and Swift 2020;Borusyak, Hull, and Jaravel 2022) and construct an instrumental variable, called HC Bartik, that captures the variation in a firm’s HC distance due to changes in the workforce composition of firms in the sector of entry.3The identification assumption for this approach is that long-term changes in the human capital of incumbents in the sector of entry do not correlate with unobserved determinants of firms’ decisions to build or buy.4Consistent with our main results, we find that firms buy when changes in incumbents’ workforces make them more distant to the sector of entry.

Next, we show that firms develop human capital by hiring new workers when they build, and by absorbing their target’s workforce when they buy. Firms that build hire more workers when they enter distant sectors than firms that buy. New workers are concentrated among the top five occupations for the sector of entry, allowing firms that build to reduce their HC distance to the new sector. In line with the idea that firms need to integrate new workers when they build, new workers come from outside the firm rather than from other subsidiaries of the firm. Firms that buy retain workers in the top five occupations for the entry sector and downsize employment in other occupations more aggressively in distant sectors.5

We provide additional evidence for a human capital channel by showing that labor adjustment costs affect the decision to build or buy through the cost of building human capital. First, we find that firms are more likely to buy than to build in distant sectors when workers in key occupations for the sector of entry are in short supply in the local labor market (LLM). Second, firms are more likely to buy than to build in distant sectors when their hiring flexibility is low due to a high percentage of permanent contracts versus temporary contracts in their workforce.

We then study the role of organizational skills in firms’ ability to develop their human capital. If firms need organizational skills to integrate new workers, then those with limited organizational skills should be more likely to buy than to build in distant sectors as building is relatively more costly in these sectors. As a result, the HC distance to the new sector should affect firms’ decision to build or buy more when they lack organizational skills. Moreover, firms should invest more in organizational skills when they build in more distant sectors than in other sectors.

We use three distinct measures of organizational skills to test the above predictions. These measures respectively capture the importance of managers in the firm (Caliendo, Monte, and Rossi-Hansberg 2015), selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses (Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou 2013;Li, Qiu, and Shen 2018) and the presence of human resources (HR) employees (Hellmann and Puri 2002). In line with our predictions, we find that the relationship between HC distance and the decision to build or buy is driven by firms with low-level organizational skills and that firms invest more in organizational skills when they build in more distant sectors.6

Finally, we explore how adjustment costs affect firms’ performance in the new sector. If building human capital takes time, then firms that build should durably underperform when they enter distant sectors, especially when they have limited organizational skills. In contrast, if buying allows firms to acquire operational human capital, then HC distance should not affect performance in the new sector. In line with these predictions, we find that firms that enter distant sectors generate significantly lower sales in the first 3 years after entry when they build. In contrast, the HC distance to the sector of entry does not predict performance when they buy, such that firms that build in distant sectors durably underperform compared to firms that buy. This pattern is more pronounced for firms with limited organizational skills. These findings suggest that buying allows firms to avoid the costs associated with building human capital and to be profitable in new sectors more quickly.

Our results contribute to the corporate diversification literature, which has focused on whether and where but not how firms diversify (seeMaksimovic and Phillips 2013, for a review of the literature). Most related to our paper,Hoberg and Phillips (2018,forthcoming),Boehm, Dhingra, and Morrow (2022), andTate and Yang (2024)study the decision to diversify and show that firms operate in sectors that require similar inputs to exploit their existing resources. The novelty of this paper is to study how firms acquire new resources to diversify their activities. We find that firms use M&As to acquire the resources that they cannot build organically. By highlighting the integration of new workers as a key adjustment cost for the acquisition of human capital, our paper is also linked to the organizational economics literature (e.g.,Prescott and Visscher 1980;Atkeson and Kehoe 2005;Lustig, Syverson, and Van Nieuwerburgh 2011;Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou 2013). Specifically, our results show that integrating new workers to exiting workforces is an obstacle to corporate diversification, especially for firms with limited organizational skills. These results imply that organizational skills allow firms not only to be more efficient in their current activities but also to enter new sectors profitably, in line with the idea that organizational skills are nonrival (Crouzet et al. 2022).7

Our paper also adds to the growing literature on the role of human capital in M&As. The novelty of our approach is that it considers organic entries as an alternative to M&As. Our contribution is threefold. First, our analysis shows that firms buy in sectors that are not too distant in terms of human capital, but more distant than are the sectors in which firms build. Thus, our paper reconciles the existing evidence that diversifying M&As are more likely to occur between firms that share similar human capital (Rhodes-Kropf and Robinson 2008;Lee, Mauer, and Xu 2018;Tate and Yang 2024) and other evidence that firms buy to acquire the human capital they lack (Ouimet and Zarutskie 2020).8Second, our findings rationalize why firms buy even in the absence of exogenous barriers to hiring and shed light on the role of organizational skills in building human capital.9Third, our results highlight a new labor-related motive for M&As beyond cost-cutting (Dessaint, Golubov, and Volpin 2017;He and le Maire fortcoming; Lagaras forthcoming), the expansion of internal labor markets (Tate and Yang 2015,2024), or the acquisition of skilled workers (Ouimet and Zarutskie 2020;Chen, Gao, and Ma 2021;Chen, Hshieh, and Zhang 2022). We show that organizational skills are required for firms to build human capital and that buying allows them to acquire already operational human capital when they lack such skills. As a result, firms with similar human capital but different organizational skills tend to follow very different M&A strategies.

Finally, our paper relates to the literature on the determinants of the decision to build or buy across sectors (McCardle and Viswanathan 1994;Phillips and Zhdanov 2013;Bernard, Redding, and Schott 2010). We contribute to this literature by studying the choice to build or buy using an exhaustive data set covering all French firms that diversify. We document that buy entries represent a very small portion of entries in new sectors, suggesting that firms buy only when they cannot build. Moreover, leveraging the exhaustive nature of the data set, we provide evidence that the effect of human capital on the decision to build or buy is driven by small firms. This finding suggests that small firms use M&As to expand their scope, thereby compensating for their lack of organizational skills, to develop human capital organically. This paper, therefore, sheds new light on the relatively underexplored diversification decisions of small, private firms (e.g.,Maksimovic and Phillips 2013;Matvos, Seru, and Silva 2018). More generally, our paper relates to a body of work exploring how firms invest in intangible capital throughout their life cycle (e.g.,Hellmann and Puri 2002;Atkeson and Kehoe 2005;Ayyagari, Demirgüç-Kunt, and Maksimovic 2024).
Our data set consists of a combination of commercial M&A data and administrative data available at the French National Institute of Statistics and Economic Studies (INSEE).

1.1. Data sources and main variables

1.1.1. M&A deals.

We collect data on M&A deals occurring between January 2003 and December 2014 from SDC Platinum and Bureau van Dijk’s Zephyr. We retain deals that involve a French acquiror and a French target to be able to match them with the French administrative data. We focus on majority deals in which the acquiror owns strictly less than 50% of the target shares before and at least 50% after the acquisition.10Our final sample includes 7,165 deals involving 4,139 acquiring firms from 2003 to 2014.

1.1.2. French administrative data sets.

We conduct the analysis at the business group level (thereafter “firm”), as decisions to diversify are made at this level and may involve resource reallocation within the group. We use the ownership link data set (Enquête sur les Liaisons financières entre sociétés(LIFI)) to retrieve the structure of firms and identify all entities linked to the diversifying firm and all the subsidiaries of companies targeted in M&A deals.11

The tax files (Bénéfices Industriels et Commerciaux) provide balance sheets and income statements at the subsidiary level.12We also retrieve a firm’s main sector of activity from the tax files and define sectors at the three-digit industry level of the French classification of sectors, which include 272 different sectors. Finally, we consolidate all variables at the business group level.

To identify entries in new sectors, we use the subsidiaries’ breakdown of sales by sector (Enquête Annuelle des Entreprises (EAE) from 2003 to 2007 and then Ventilation des Ventes par Activité (VAC) from 2008 to 2014). This data set records firms’ comprehensive sales amounts realized in every sector in every year.13

We also consolidate the breakdown of sales and define entries at the business group level. A firm enters a new sector in a given year when its sales in this sector are positive for the first time in that year across all of its subsidiaries. A firm “buys” if it enters the new sector by acquiring a new subsidiary. In contrast, a firm “builds” if the entering subsidiary was already controlled by the firm before entry or was created the year of the entry. We impose an entry threshold to ensure that the entry is economically meaningful from the firm perspective. We require that sales in the new sector represent at least 1% of the firm’s total sales for the year before entry.14

1.1.3. Matched employer-employee data set.

We use the French matched employer–employee data set (Déclarations Annuelles des Données Sociales(DADS)) to observe firms’ workforce composition. When filing yearly payroll taxes, employers are required to report the following detailed information about their workers: gross and net wages, the number of hours worked, the type of contract, and a detailed occupation code for each worker. Occupations are reported as four-digit code occupations (Nomenclatures des professions et catégories socio-professionnelles des emplois salariés des employeurs privés et publics(PCS-ESE)). There are 414 different occupations at this level, including, for instance, 28 different types of engineers (e.g., logistics, IT, electrical, or mechanical engineers).15

We use this information to construct a firm-level measure of HC distance to the sector of entry as follows:

1.2. Descriptive statistics

Panel A ofTable 1presents the evolution of the proportion of build and buy entries between 2005 and 2014. While at the beginning of the period, approximately 1.3% of entries are made by acquisition, this figure increases over the sample period, reaching 2.25% of total entries in 2014. Buy entries are, on average, larger than build entries. When weighting by entry sales, buy entries represent 15.68% of total entries. The proportion of buy entries remains stable at 1.4%–1.8% when using the other levels of industry classification (panel B).

Table 2reports the summary statistics of the variables used in our analysis. We find that the average HC distance between a diversifying firm and the sector of entry is 0.79, suggesting that the average firm lacks workers in occupations that are key to operating in the sector of entry. Our baseline measure of HC distance is based on the overlap between the shares of the diversifying firms’ wage bill going to each worker occupation and those of incumbents in the sector of entry. The distribution of HC distance remains stable when we use the shares of firms’ workers and the shares of the number of hours worked in the firm as alternative specifications (Internet AppendixTable B2). Instead of taking the distance to the representative firm in the sector of entry, we also calculate the average HC distance between the diversifying firm and incumbents. The (simple or weighted) average HC distances are larger than the baseline measure, suggesting that incumbents in the sector of entry exhibit significant heterogeneity in their HC composition.17

The average firm in our sample employs 45 workers hired in 16.7 different occupations. It generates approximately €60,000 in value added per worker, owns €70,000 in tangible assets per worker, and holds €30,000 in cash per worker. The average firm invests €85,000 at the time of entry. Of the diversifying firms, 55% were already diversified before entry into the new sector. Only 2% of them include a listed subsidiary in the business group.

On average, firms report €0.5 million in sales in the sector of entry in the first year, with a large dispersion around the mean. Moreover, 16% of the entries identified at the three-digit level of the French Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) happen to be in the same two-digit industry, and 33% happen to be in the same one-digit industry. In addition, we find that 23% of the entries occur in upstream sectors, while 16% occur in downstream sectors. Only 3% of the entries occur in a differentrégion(a coarser administrative area), and 6% occur in a differentdépartement(a finer administrative area).
article.2. Main Results:
2.1. Human capital and the decision to build or buy

2.1.1. Empirical specification.

Why do some firms enter a new sector by building on their existing human capital (“build”) while others buy an incumbent company (“buy”)? Our main hypothesis is that building human capital involves not only hiring but also integrating (i.e., training, managing, and assigning to tasks) new workers into the workforce. Under this assumption, the cost of building should increase with the level of required investment in human capital, that is, with the distance to the new sector. Therefore, we expect firms to buy in distant sectors as buying allows them to acquire their target’s already operational human capital and save on integration costs. Conversely, if firms could simply expand their scope and build human capital by hiring new workers, the HC distance to the new sector should be irrelevant in the decision to build or buy, as firms could acquire human capital by poaching workers from incumbent companies.

Our baseline specification tests whether firms are more likely to buy when their human capital is distant from the sector of entry and to build when their human capital is close:

1(Buy) g , n , t = λ n , o , t + δ HC distance g , n , t − 1 + β X g , n , t − 1 + ε g , n , t , (1)

where the dependent variable1(Buy)g,n,tis a dummy variable equal to one if firmgbuys an incumbent company in the sector of entrynin yeartand equal to zero if the firm builds in sectorn⁠. The main independent variableHC Distanceg,n,t−1is our measure of the HC distance between firmgand the sector of entrynin yeart−1⁠. The vector of control variablesXg,n,t−1includes other firm characteristics that may influence the choice to build or buy. The control variables include firm size, measured as the total (log) number of workers, value added, number of occupations, total cash holdings, tangible assets, and total wages. We scale all control variables, except for size, by the number of workers.Internet Appendix Aincludes our variable definitions.

λn,o,tis the sector of origin×entry×year fixed effects, which allows us to compare firms operating in the same sector of originoand entering the same new sectornin the same yeartwhile also controlling for unobservable synergies between the sector of origin and that of entry, as well as any unobservable factors related to the sector of entry (e.g., fixed costs and barriers to entry) or the sector of origin (e.g., demand shocks) that can vary over time. We double-cluster standard errors at the levels of sector of origin and sector of entry to account for correlations of errors across firms within the sector of origin and sector of entry, respectively.

To mitigate the role of omitted variables in the decision to build or buy, we construct a matched sample of firms based on the sector of origin, the sector of entry, and quartiles of firm size before entry, measured by realized sales in the sector of origin in the year before entry. In an alternative specification, we add firm fixed effects inEquation (1)to exploit the variation in HC distance within firms across sectors.

2.1.2. Results.

Table 3reports the results with different specifications and sets of fixed effects. The baseline specification in column 1 includes sector of origin×sector of entry×year fixed effects. We show that firms’ HC distance to the sector of entry is positively correlated with their probability of acquiring an incumbent in that sector. A one-standard-deviation increase in HC distance is associated with a 0.39-percentage-point increase in the probability of buying (column 1). This relationship is sizable, equal to approximately 20% of the unconditional probability of buying, and significant at the 5% level. Regarding the control variables, we find that large firms, firms with a more diverse mix of occupations, and less financially constrained firms are more likely to buy compared to other firms.

In columns 2 and 3, we test the robustness of our main findings to different combinations of fixed effects. In column 2, we include the interacted sector of origin×entry×quartile of firm size fixed effects to compare firms of similar size in the sector of origin that enter the same sector. In column 3, we compare firms that initially operate in the same main sector of origin and the same secondary sector of origin and enter the same sector. This specification accounts for unobservable synergies between the firms’ main and secondary sectors of activity and the sector of entry. The HC distance coefficient is robust to these alternative fixed effects combinations, and its value remains very similar in these specifications.

In column 4, we add firm and year fixed effects in addition to the interacted sector fixed effects. The HC distance coefficient remains positive and significant at the 5% level, suggesting that within the same firm, we observe build entries in close sectors and buy entries in distant sectors. This result shows that the positive relationship between buy entries and HC distance cannot be explained by firm-level omitted variables.18

2.1.3. Robustness tests.

Alternative measures of HC distance.InInternet AppendixTable B2, we test the robustness of our main findings to alternative measures of HC distance. In columns 2 and 3, we define a firm’s HC distance as the cosine distance between the vectors of the number of workers and the number of hours worked by occupation, as our baseline measure (column 1) may overestimate the weight of a few high-paid individuals (e.g., CEOs) and underestimate the weights of numerically important occupations (e.g., part-time workers). In columns 4 to 6, we define HC distance as the average distance (equally weighted, sales-weighted, and workforce-weighted) between the diversifying firm and incumbents in the sector of entry. In column 7, we capture the differences in skills within occupations by calculating the HC distance between the wage bill compositions of the firm and incumbent firms across occupation×wage terciles. We find that our results are robust to the use of these alternative measures.

Alternative thresholds in the sector of entry.InInternet AppendixTable B3, we test the robustness of our results to the definition of an entry in a new sector. In column 1, we do not impose any minimal entry size. In columns 2 to 4, we impose entry size thresholds equal to 1% (baseline), 5%, and 10%, respectively. In column 5, we run a weighted least squares regression, where observations are weighted by entry sales. The magnitude of the effect of HC distance on buy entries is economically and statistically stronger as we impose a higher threshold of entry sales, suggesting that the decision to buy or build is more sensitive to HC distance when entry in the new sector is economically relevant.

Sector-level HC distance.InInternet AppendixTable B4, we compute the total number of build and buy entries by pair of sector of origin and sector of entry and calculate a sector-level HC distance between the sector of entry and the sector of origin. Column 1 shows that most entries occur between sectors with similar human capital. Columns 2 and 3 show that both build and buy entries are less likely between sectors with long HC distance. This effect is stronger for build entries than for buy entries. In column 4, we find that the fraction of buy entries compared to that of build entries increases with increasing sector-level HC distance.

2.2. Shift-share design

2.2.1. Empirical specification.

Although our specification includes detailed firm-level control variables and granular fixed effects, unobservable variables associated with both the distance to the sector of entry and the decision to build or buy can still act as confounding factors. For instance, firms may anticipate the mode of entry and hire workers in key occupations before entering the new sector.

To establish a causal link between firms’ human capital and their decision to build or buy, we exploit long-term changes in incumbent firms’ human capital in the entry sector. The workforce composition of French firms experienced significant changes during our sample period, specifically because of the increasing importance of IT (Harrigan, Reshef, and Toubal 2021;Lashkari, Bauer, and Boussard 2024). We construct an instrument, called HC Bartik, that captures the variation in HC distance between a firm and a sector of entry that is due to changes in incumbent firms’ human capital over time. To do so, we hold constant diversifying firms’ workforce composition at the beginning of the sample period, in 2003. Then, we isolate variations in HC distance due to changes in incumbents’ workforce composition over time.

We define the Bartik instrument as follows:

2.2.2. Results.

Column 1 ofTable 4shows that firms are more likely to build in sectors that have become closer over time than in more distant sectors.23A one-standard-deviation increase inHC Bartikis associated with a 0.26-percentage-point decrease in the probability of buying. This relationship is equal to approximately 13% of the unconditional probability of buying and significant at the 5% level. This result holds if we replace sector of origin×entry fixed effects with sector of origin×entry×year fixed effects as in the main specification (column 2). In column 3, we check that the firm’s past human capital as of 2003 cannot solely predict the mode of entry, which would be the case if incumbents’ human capital were highly persistent over time. We show that controlling for HC distance as of 2003 does not affect the coefficient of interest, suggesting that the variation in incumbents’ human capital is critical over time for its identification.

Consistent with the increase in the number of IT occupations, we find that theHC Bartikcoefficient is significant only in sectors in which the share of the wage bill of IT workers increased more than the sample median between 2003 and 2011 (columns 4 and 5). This finding implies that the variation in human capital on which we rely for identification is linked to the rise in the use of IT at the beginning of the 2000s (e.g.,Lashkari, Bauer, and Boussard 2024).

2.2.3. Robustness checks.

Firms with IT occupations in 2003.Since our instrument exploits the rise of IT occupations, a potential concern is that firms that already employed IT workers in 2003 differ on unobservable dimensions linked to their decision to build or buy. Hence, the negativeHC Bartikcoefficient would not reflect the fact that firms build more in sectors that became closer to them but instead that firms with IT workers in 2003 built more in sectors that experienced a rise in IT during the sample period. We provide two pieces of evidence to rule out this concern. First, in column 6 we show that the coefficient of interest remains unchanged even after controlling for a dummy equal to one if the firm was employing IT workers in 2003. Second, inInternet AppendixTable B7, we regress shocks on potential observable confounders (Borusyak, Hull, and Jaravel 2022). We find that the shocksΔs^n,i,03,11are balanced across all observable firm-level characteristicsXg,n,2003⁠.

Merger waves.Given that mergers occur in waves, another potential issue is thatHC Bartikmay capture variations in different firms’ exposure to merger waves during the period 2003–2011. We show that our results remain the same after we control for the average number of acquisitions coming from the sectors of origin in 2003 to the sector of entry (column 7).

Endogenous shares.The identification source on which we rely for our Bartik instrument is to use the shocks as the source of identification and the shares as weights for the instruments (Borusyak, Hull, and Jaravel 2022). We do not use the shares as a source of identification as proposed inGoldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin, and Swift (2020)because these shares reflect the endogenous decisions of firms. Nevertheless, to address potential concerns about the endogeneity of shares, we replace the sharess^g,i,03inEquation (2)with a dummy equal to one if the firm hires any worker in occupationi⁠, and zero otherwise (column 8). The endogeneity problem is likely less problematic for this dummy than for the shares themselves. The results are the same regardless of whether we use dummies instead of shares (column 8 and column 1, respectively).
article.3. Human Capital, Labor Adjustments, and:
3.1. Employment growth around build and buy entries

We study employment growth around entries in the new sector to understand how firms develop their human capital when they build and when they buy.

First, we conduct an event study around the year of entry in the new sector. The graph aboveTable 5plots the evolution of employment growth from yeart−4to yeart+3of the year of entry for firms with an HC distance larger than the sample median in each sector of origin×entry×year triplet.24

Firms that buy experience lower employment growth when they enter distant sectors (black curve). In contrast, for firms that build experience relatively greater employment growth increases when they enter more distant sectors (light grey curve). The difference is statistically significant. Importantly, we do not find any relationship between HC distance and employment growth for firms that build or buy before entry, suggesting that labor adjustments occur after firms enter the new sector.

Second,Table 5reports postentry labor adjustments.25In column 1, we regress employment growth from yeart−1tot+3on HC distance for firms that build (panel A) and for firms that buy (panel B), and we test whether the coefficients are significantly different across panels in the last row. We find that a one-standard-deviation increase in preentry HC distance is associated with a 0.4 p.p. increase in employment growth (1.1% increase from the unconditional mean) around the entry in the new sector for firms that build and a 3.3 p.p. decrease for firms that buy (9.4% increase from the unconditional mean). The difference between the coefficients is significant at the 10% level.

In columns 2 and 3, we break down firms’ employment growth between the five most important occupations for the sector of entry (in terms of shares of the wage bill) and other occupations. Firms that build hire workers with the right set of skills for the sector of entry when they enter more distant sectors (column 2). In contrast, firms do not hire more in distant sectors when they buy, consistent with the idea that the workers employed by the target already possess the right set of skills. The difference is significant at the 1% level.

Column 3 shows that firms that build grow less in nonessential occupations in distant sectors than in other sectors. Firms that buy also downsize their employment more aggressively in distant sectors. Hence, they retain workers in key occupations while laying off those in nonessential occupations. Finally, in column 4, we show that firms that enter distant sectors experience a greater reduction in the HC distance to the sector of entry betweentandt+3⁠, regardless of the mode of entry.

Overall, the results show that firms develop their human capital by hiring new workers when they build and by absorbing their target’s workforce when they buy.

3.2. Internal and external labor markets

Do new hires come from internal or external labor markets? The cost of integrating new workers is likely higher when they are hired on external labor markets than on internal labor markets. InTable 6, we decompose employment growth around entries into employment growth coming from internal labor markets (i.e., labor flows from another subsidiary of the firm) and from external labor markets (i.e., labor flows from outside the firm). We regress the employment growth from yeart−1to yeart+3on HC distance for firms that build (panel A) and for firms that buy (panel B) and test for the difference between firms that build and buy in the last row. Our specifications include sector of origin, sector of entry, and year fixed effects. Standard errors are double-clustered at the levels of sector of origin and sector of entry.

Column 1 reproduces the results on employment growth inTable 5. Column 2 shows that firms with greater HC distance to the sector of entry are not significantly more likely to hire workers from other subsidiaries of the firm. This finding underscores the limited role internal labor markets play in hiring new workers. Conversely, column 3 shows that firms tend to hire externally when they build and let workers go when they buy. A one-standard-deviation increase in preentry HC distance is associated with a 0.5 p.p. increase in external hires around the entry in the new sector for firms that build and a 4.7 p.p. decrease for firms that buy, which are equal to 1.4% and 13.4%, respectively, of the average growth in the number of external hires. The difference between these coefficients is significant at the 1% level (last row of column 3).

These results show that new workers are hired externally rather than internally transferred from other firm subsidiaries, suggesting that firms that build need to incur costs to integrate new workers. In contrast, the limited role for internal labor markets suggest that firms that buy do not largely assimilate their newly acquired workforce into theirs.

3.3. Labor adjustment costs and building human capital

We provide two additional pieces of evidence supporting the notion that labor adjustment frictions affect the decision to build or buy. First, we test the hypothesis that building human capital is more costly when key occupations for the sector of entry are in short supply in local labor markets in which the firm operates. This test relies on occupation-level data obtained from the French unemployment agency (Pôle emploi) to create a measure of LLM tightness based on the local availability of workers in key occupations for the new sector.26

We estimate our main equation,Equation (1), on subsamples corresponding to terciles of LLM tightness. We add LLM fixed effects to control for fixed geographical variation in the likelihood of building or buying. The first tercile corresponds to LLMs, where firms can easily find workers in key occupations for the sector of entry. The third tercile corresponds to tight LLMs, where key occupations for the sector of entry are in short supply. In panel A ofTable 7, we show that firms are more likely to buy in distant sectors when LLMs are tight, as it is relatively costly to find workers in the external job market.

Second, we leverage the fact that firms that rely on permanent contracts are more reluctant to hire because of the stringent legal protection afforded to such contracts (Schivardi and Torrini 2008). Hence, building human capital in distant sectors is likely to be more costly when firms’ hiring flexibility is low. The following two types of labor contracts exist in France: temporary (Contrats a Durée Determinée) and permanent (Contrats a Durée Indeterminée). We proxy for a firm’s cost of building human capital by the fraction of workers employed through permanent contracts versus temporary contracts. We measure the likelihood of using permanent contracts for the first time the firm appears in the sample (that is, 4.9 years before entry on average) to limit the role of anticipation and split the sample into three terciles. Panel B ofTable 7reports the results. We find that HC distance to the sector of entry affects the decision to build or buy more for firms with a greater percentage of permanent contracts. This result supports the idea that firms are more likely to buy in distant sectors when building human capital is more costly.

3.4. Organizational skills and building human capital

Why do firms buy in distant sectors instead of building? If firms could easily expand their scope and build human capital by hiring new workers, then human capital should be irrelevant in the decision to build or buy. The fact that the distance to the sector of entry affects the decision to build or buy suggests instead that building human capital requires more than just hiring new workers.

The organizational economics literature suggests that firms need to train, deploy, and assign new workers to tasks after hiring them, which requires organizational skills (Prescott and Visscher 1980). This hypothesis generates two empirical predictions. First, firms with limited organizational skills should be more likely to buy in distant sectors, as building human capital is more costly. Second, firms should invest more in organizational skills when they build in distant sectors since they need to invest more in human capital when entering these sectors.

We leverage three measures of organizational skills from the literature to test these predictions. We use the relative importance of managers in the firm (i.e., the share of the wage bill that goes to workers in the top layers of the hierarchy,Caliendo, Monte, and Rossi-Hansberg 2015), SG&A expenses (Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou 2013;Li, Qiu, and Shen 2018), and the presence of HR employees (Hellmann and Puri 2002).27

InTable 8, we test whether firms with limited organizational skills are more likely to buy in distant sectors than in other sectors. Columns 1 to 3 report the results of the sample split by terciles of our first measure. The results show that the relationship between HC distance and the decision to build or buy is significant only for firms in which top managers receive a small fraction of the wage bill (column 1). Similarly, in columns 4 to 6, we find that firms with a small fraction of the wage bill dedicated to SG&A occupations drive the relationship between HC distance and the decision to build or buy. Finally, columns 7 and 8 show that the effect of HC on the decision to build or buy is observed only for firms that do not employ any HR workers. The coefficient magnitudes are similar across specifications and are only slightly smaller than those in our main table,Table 3, implying that most of the effect of HC distance on the decision to build or buy is driven by firms with limited organizational skills. Our findings support the idea that firms buy in distant sectors when they lack organizational skills, as buying allows them to acquire operational human capital from their target.

Among build entries, we find that investment in organizational skills is positively and significantly related to HC distance for all three measures. Column 1 shows that a one-standard-deviation increase in preentry HC distance is associated with a 6.6 p.p. increase in the fraction of the wage bill dedicated to SG&A occupations for firms that build, which is equal to 11.9% of the unconditional mean and significant at the 1% level. In contrast, the distance to sector of entry is not related to investment in organizational capital for buy entries. This finding is consistent with the idea that firms do not need to invest in organizational skills when buying as they acquire operational human capital. The difference in coefficients between build and buy entries is significant at the 1% level (last row of column 1).

Overall, the results support the idea that building human capital requires integrating new workers, which makes it more costly for firms to build in distant sectors.

3.5. Building human capital and firm size

Should the effect of human capital on the decision to build or buy vary across firms of different sizes? Although acquisitions are more prevalent for large firms, this section shows that small firms are more likely to buy in distant sectors, as they typically face higher costs of building human capital.

InTable 10, we interact HC distance with the following measures of size: terciles of firm employment size (column 1), value added (column 2), tangible assets (column 3), a dummy variable capturing whether the firm is already diversified (column 4), and whether the firm is publicly listed (column 5).28In columns 1 to 3, we find that the coefficients on the interactions between the second and third terciles of firm size and HC distance are negative, suggesting that the effect of HC distance on the decision to build or buy is stronger for small firms. A one-standard-deviation increase in HC distance is associated with a 0.56 p.p. increase in the probability of buying for firms in the first tercile in terms of number of workers, as compared to a 0.26 p.p. increase for firms in the second tercile and a 0.18 p.p. increase for firms in the third tercile (column 1), and these relationships are equal to 28%, 13%, and 9% of the unconditional probability of buying, respectively. In column 4, we find that HC distance is significantly more important for firms not already diversified. Finally, in column 5, we find that listed firms are more likely to buy, although the interaction with HC distance shows that listed firms are not significantly more likely to build in more distant sectors.

Overall, our results show that small firms drive the relationship between human capital and the decision to build or buy. This is consistent with the idea that small firms face higher costs to build as they have low organizational skills (e.g.,Atkeson and Kehoe 2005;Caliendo, Monte, and Rossi-Hansberg 2015;Hellmann and Puri 2002). In contrast, the distance to the sector of entry is irrelevant for larger firms, which already have the organizational skills needed to build human capital.

3.6. Building human capital and post-entry performance

Building human capital takes time (Atkeson and Kehoe 2005). In this section, we study how the cost of building human capital affect firms’ performance in the new sector. Specifically, we hypothesize that firms that build in distant sectors predictably underperform, especially when they have limited organizational skills. In contrast, buying allows firms to acquire already operational human capital, meaning that HC distance should not affect the performance of firms that buy in the new sector.

The graph aboveTable 11shows that the distance to the sector of entry affects firms’ performance differently depending on whether they build or buy.29The black curve plots the sales dynamics of buy entries and shows that they are not significantly related to HC distance. The light grey curve shows that firms that build in distant sectors tend to underperform at least until 3 years after entering the new sector. The difference between build and buy entries is statistically significant.

We further analyze the effects of HC distance on firms’ postentry performance inTable 11. In column 1, we find that postentry sales decrease with HC distance for firms that build (panel A). In contrast, entry sales are unrelated to HC distance for firms that buy (panel B). A one-standard-deviation increase in the firm’s HC distance in the year before entry is associated with 23% lower sales in the first year of entry for firms that build. This difference between firms that build or buy is significant at the 1% level. In contrast, the impact of HC distance on sales growth is not significantly different for build versus buy entries (column 2), which is consistent with the fact that the difference in performance in distant sectors is persistent over time. In column 3, we show that firms that build in distant sectors are less likely to survive for at least 3 years. We do not find such an effect for firms that buy, although the difference in the coefficient is not statistically significant.

We also study whether the cost of building human capital affects not only performance in the new sector, but also firms’ overall performance. We consider the following two measures of firm performance: the change in operating income per worker (column 4), and return on assets (column 5) over the 3 years following entry. We also find that HC distance is associated with lower-level performance when the firm builds. The coefficient is also negative for buy entries but is imprecisely estimated due to the small sample size.

This finding supports the idea that buying allows firms to enter distant sectors faster than does building, as acquiring already operational human capital allows them to operate efficiently upon entering the new sector.

InTable 12, we test the effect of organizational skills on post entry performance. For all three measures of organizational skills, we find that the negative relationship between HC distance and postentry sales of firms that build is less pronounced when the firm has high-level organizational skills. In column 1, a one-standard-deviation increase in HC distance is associated with 30% lower sales for firms that build with low-level organizational skills, as compared to 20% lower sales for firms that build with high-level organizational skills. This difference in entry performance between firms with different levels of organizational skills is significant at the 5% level in columns 1 and 2, and at the 1% level in column 3. In columns 4 to 6, in contrast, we do not find that the post-merger performance of firms that buy is negatively affected by HC distance, even when they have low-level organizational skills.30

Taken together, our results suggest that building human capital requires organizational skills, and that buying allows firms to avoid the costs associated with building and be profitable in new sectors more quickly.
article.4. Conclusion:
This paper studies how firms develop the human capital they need to enter new sectors. We study the trade-off between building human capital organically and acquiring an incumbent firm’s human capital to shed light on the frictions that firms face in expanding their scope.

Using French administrative data sets linked to commercial M&A data sets, we document that most firms enter new sectors by building on their existing resources, suggesting that firms buy only when they cannot build. In line with the presence of integration costs for human capital, our main result is that firms are more likely to buy when they lack the human capital needed to operate in the new sector. We establish causality by exploiting variations in firms’ distance to the sector of entry due to changes in the workforce composition of incumbent firms.

Consistent with the organizational economics literature, we find that building human capital requires organizational skills (i.e., to have the capacity to integrate new workers): the distance to the sector of entry is especially important for firms with limited organizational skills, and firms invest more in organizational skills when building in more distant sectors than in other sectors. In line with the idea that building human capital takes time, firms that build in distant sectors durably underperform. In contrast, the distance to the sector of entry does not predict firms’ performance when they buy, suggesting that buying allows firms to avoid the costs associated with building human capital and to be profitable in new sectors more quickly.

Overall, this paper contributes to the literature by shedding light on the determinants of corporate diversification and, by extension, firm growth. Our results show that building human capital is an obstacle to diversification because it requires organizational skills. These results imply that organizational skills allow firms to not only be more efficient in their current activities but also to profitably enter new sectors.

This is the largest change in wage shares of the wage bill across all occupations in the Business services sector. Our instrument predicts that firms with a high share of IT workers in their wage bill at the beginning of our sample (the “share”) should see a decrease in their HC distance to the Business services sector.

We contribute to that literature by showing how firms invest in organizational skills and how these skills influence firms’ diversification strategies.

However, this literature has been mostly silent on how firms acquire new assets and resources beyond M&As.

10 SDC Platinum and Bureau van Dijk’s Zephyr do not provide standardized French firm identifiers. We proceed in several steps to retrieve firm identifiers that allow us to link M&A deals to the French administrative data set. First, we use tickers (available only for publicly traded firms) and Bureau van Dijk identifiers (available only for deals in Zephyr). Second, we search for firms’ names and addresses on the following two specialized websites: www.bodacc.fr , a government website that reports official notifications, and www.societe.com , a commercial website that aggregates information about French companies. Third, we drop companies for which the Jaró-Winkler string distance between the original name and the name on these websites is less than 0.8. Fourth, we manually check the resulting matches. Finally, we exclude leveraged buyouts and private equity deals from the sample.

11 In addition, we check that the target of an M&A deal in our sample becomes one of the acquiror’s subsidiaries after the date of the M&A deal, as registered in Zephyr/SDC Platinum. If a discrepancy exists, we replace the date of the M&A deal with the date of the effective link observed in the ownership link data set. If the gap between the event date and the link date is larger than 2 years, we discard the M&A deal and the involved firms from the sample.

12 We exclude from our sample firms in the financial, agricultural, and public sectors because they follow different accounting standards from firms in other sectors. We focus on firms using the standard tax regime ( Régime normal ) and exclude firms using the simplified regime ( Régime simplifié ) because some important information is not reported by the latter firms.

13 The survey covers 85% of aggregate sales and 96% of sales in the manufacturing sector. The data are exhaustive for firms with at least 20 workers and randomly include smaller firms. To avoid false positives, we also discard those entries that may in fact correspond to increased coverage of the LIFI or VAC data sets. First, we discard entries due to changes in ownership links in LIFI for which we do not find a corresponding M&A transaction in SDC Platinum or Zephyr. Second, we discard entries due to a subsidiary entering the VAC data set.

14 We also ensure that buy entries are not the byproduct of horizontal acquisitions by removing buy entries in which the acquirer and target realize more than 50% of their sales in the same sectors before acquisition.

The presence of HR employees is a dummy variable equal to one if the firm employs at least one HR worker. We use a dummy variable as 77% of the firms in our sample do not have in-house HR workers.

30 Several factors can explain this finding. First, the literature presents mixed evidence on the impact of organizational capital on merger outcomes. While M&As appear to generate more synergies when acquirers have more organizational capital ( Li, Qiu, and Shen 2018 ), the success of integration also depends heavily on the degree of collaboration between the acquirer and the target’s workers after the merger ( Fulghieri and Sevilir 2021 ; Li and Wang 2023 ). Second, our analysis focuses solely on the acquirer’s organizational skills and abstract from variations in target firms’ organizational capital level. Third, our analysis in Table 6 shows that postmerger employment growth stems from external hiring rather than from internal reallocation of workers, implying limited interactions between the acquirer’s and target’s workforces. One interpretation for this absence of postmerger integration is that our sample focuses on diversified acquisitions, for which the assimilation of the target’s workforce may be less important for the merger to be a success. Finally, the lack of statistical power may also contribute to the nonsignificant results.
